slides.title: Rumaisa Habib: Formalizing Dependence of Web Infrastructure
Diwen Xue: Fingerprinting Deep Packet Inspection Devices by Their Ambiguities

chat.author: Maxence Fléchier
chat.text: am i the only one for whom the slides are not loading?

chat.author: Muhammad Usama Sardar
chat.text: me too. Slides are not loading in onsite tool.

chat.author: Dave Plonka
chat.text: they are loading for me (onsite) using the full Meetecho client

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: when she's saying 'provider' does she mean web site?

chat.author: John Scudder
chat.text: Did I correctly understand that EMD normalizes to “everyone is self-hosted”?

chat.author: Ike Kunze
chat.text: re slides not showing in onsite tool: probably due to the fact that it's a screen share, not a slide share.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: Does having a long tail of thousands of providers that are each infinitesimally tiny make a difference to the EMD versus a small number of providers where the smallest one is equal to the same of the long tail?

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text:
Stephen Farrell said:

when she's saying 'provider' does she mean web site?

I thought she meant SDN or hosting service.

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: seems a bit of a mix of ASes/web properties

chat.author: Junxiao Shi
chat.text: The term provider refers to a hosting provider.

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: thanks - I've not read the paper obviously:-)

chat.author: Paolo Saviano
chat.text:
re slides not showing in onsite tool: probably due to the fact that it's a screen share, not a slide share.

correct

chat.author: Dirk Kutscher
chat.text: yes

chat.author: Dirk Kutscher
chat.text: Slides in the datatracker: https://datatracker.ietf.org/meeting/126/materials/slides-126-irtfopen-rumaisa-habib-formalizing-dependence-of-web-infrastructure-00

chat.author: Junxiao Shi
chat.text:
DDoS is the cause of Cloudflarization.
I used to have A+AAAA record directly to server, but a month of daily DDoS made me setup Zero Trust Tunnel.

chat.author: Dave Plonka
chat.text:
Good point, @Junxiao Shi. This also allows vulnerable sites to open up to access to their content globally. (It is not unusual for an attacked site to limit access to their country otherwise.)
"Cloudflarization" is also interesting, to me, where it gets a formerly IPv4 site's content available on IPv6. I'm interested in determining how hypergiants are responsible for IPv6 adoption w.r.t. content availablity.

chat.author: Lixia Zhang
chat.text:
this was discussed in the DIN 2021 workshop report:  "...  Again, as
time went on, increasing network security threats, such as
viruses and DDoS attacks, together with shortage of IT manpower, resulted in web services being consolidated by the
big cloud providers,..."

chat.author: Dave Plonka
chat.text: Thanks, @lixia. It is still certainly true today, 5 years hence, if not more so. (IMO)

chat.author: Brian Trammell
chat.text: this is the "how big do you have to be to make it too expensive to knock you off the web" question.

chat.author: Dave Plonka
chat.text: I like it when globalization of content and IPv6 accessibility comes along for the ride, even when not the motivation.

chat.author: Dave Plonka
chat.text: It allows intelligent evolution of hyperscalar infrastructure (like IPv6) to be easier to adopt.

chat.author: Maxence Fléchier
chat.text: the slides are slightly different though, but the gist of it is there

chat.author: Junxiao Shi
chat.text:
I don't feel guilty turning on the orange cloud.
My origin server is located in a small provider (currently Advin Servers in Malaysia), so I can switch CDN providers if necessary.

chat.author: Dave Plonka
chat.text: right... good to remind that having your own origin is a key to being multi-CDN or nimble w.r.t. CDN.

chat.author: Junxiao Shi
chat.text:
I maintain No IPv6 Hall of Shame and IPv6 Less than /64 Hall of Incompetence of the hosting providers.
I don't think it's good to rely on CDN provider for IPv6 enablement, so I try to push hosting providers to have IPv6 to each server.

chat.author: Brian Trammell
chat.text: (eliot is asking one of the questions i deferred :) )

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: we could do with a 2nd independent LE for sure

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: but that's been true for years

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: Is there a meaningful way of combining the scores of the layers for a given region? What's the significance of being dependent on country A for layer X and dependent on country B for layer Y, vs being dependent on country A for both?

chat.author: Junxiao Shi
chat.text:
2nd independent LE

There are other ACME providers available for free without manual registration.
Caddy server can acquire LetsEncrypt and ZeroSSL automatically.

chat.author: Eliot Lear
chat.text:
Jonathan Hoyland said:

Is there a meaningful way of combining the scores of the layers for a given region? What's the significance of being dependent on country A for layer X and dependent on country B for layer Y, vs being dependent on country A for both?

Or is there a hidden dependency on a single country or set of services? If country B relies on Country A....

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: @Eliot Lear So that's what I was thinking, you'd have to take the eigenvectors to figure out cascading dependencies

chat.author: Eliot Lear
chat.text: But Rumaisa's right: it doesn't work out perfectly cleanly, because it depends on where the interface seams are.

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: Yeah, I think she's right too. You could run the computation easily enough, but what would the output mean?

chat.author: Gautam Akiwate
chat.text: The _causal_ relationships would be the logical next step. For instance, we found that the choice of hosting provider often dictates the DNS provider and the CA.

chat.author: Gautam Akiwate
chat.text: But that is really hard to reason about just looking at the scores. I am sure Rumaisa is game for follow up work if you folks have thoughts :)

chat.author: Jonathan Hoyland
chat.text: That makes sense, Cloudflare has a default CA issuer for example, so the two are tied together.

chat.author: Eliot Lear
chat.text: 45 fragments?!  What the heck is the MTU?

chat.author: Junxiao Shi
chat.text: IPv4 allows fragments much smaller than MTU.

chat.author: Eliot Lear
chat.text: Right but on a 1500 byte MTU 45 fragments is 33 bytes(!).  So this was a very interesting use of fragmentation.

chat.author: Ryo Yanagida
chat.text: maybe applying a really inefficient packet pacing :joy:

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: Maybe trying to avoid DPI by pretending to be a SYN packet.

chat.author: Junxiao Shi
chat.text: In-line DPI can choose to block all fragments, similar to some DDoS mitigation devices.

chat.author: Antoine Fressancourt
chat.text: @Yoav then you end up being suspect of SYN Flood

chat.author: Yoav Nir
Yoav Nir
chat.text: But they are decrypting and re-encrypting the HTTPS traffic.

chat.author: Stephen Farrell
chat.text: well for the .ru case they're also throttling

chat.author: Yoav Nir
chat.text: Traffic is always throttled unless it's to https://www.speedtest.net
